summary:
The practice of deliberate mummification in ancient Egypt, developing systematically around 2600–2400 BC during the Old Kingdom, represents one of the most distinctive and technically sophisticated mortuary traditions in world history. While natural mummification of bodies buried in the hot, dry desert sand predates deliberate mummification, the development of intentional preservation techniques — removing internal organs, dehydrating the body with natron salt, and wrapping in linen bandages — transformed mummification into an elaborate ritual process tied to the theology of the afterlife.

The earliest deliberate mummification techniques in the Old Kingdom were relatively simple, but the practice grew increasingly sophisticated through the Middle and New Kingdoms. By the peak of the tradition around 1000 BC, the process involved removing the brain through the nostrils, extracting the viscera and storing them in canopic jars, treating the body cavity with natron and various resins, and wrapping the body in hundreds of metres of linen bandages interspersed with amulets and ritual objects. The entire process took approximately 70 days and required specialised priests and craftsmen.

Mummification was deeply integrated with Egyptian theology. The preservation of the physical body was essential to the deceased's continued existence in the afterlife, where the spirit (ka and ba) needed to return to the body. The elaborate funerary rites surrounding mummification — including the Opening of the Mouth ceremony that restored the deceased's senses — connected the practices of the living to the cosmological drama of death, judgement, and resurrection presided over by the god Osiris.

The mummies produced by ancient Egyptians have proved invaluable to modern science, providing evidence about ancient diet, disease, genetics, and material culture that would otherwise be inaccessible. CT scanning, DNA analysis, and other non-destructive modern techniques have transformed the study of Egyptian mummies, yielding insights into population history, parasitic diseases, and the lives of individuals across a wide range of social classes. Mummification as a practice continued in Egypt into the Christian period and spread in modified forms to other cultures in contact with Egypt.
